description:
Spec §6 today: at BALLS_PLUGIN_DEPTH cap, 'nested ops run PLUGIN-FREE (suppressed, not refused)' and 'a plugin may deliberately re-enable plugins on a nested call.'

What the guard actually catches is almost always a BUG: self-retrigger — a plugin wired on op X whose handler shells 'bl X' again (e.g. a sync.post plugin running 'bl sync'), climbing depth to the cap. The legit shell-back (forge claim.post doing 'bl create' for the gate child) goes 1 deep and never re-triggers its own op, so it never nears the cap. Mutual plugin1<->plugin2 is the exotic case; self-retrigger is the common one.

So 'run plugin-free at the cap, silently' is the worst option: it converts a runaway into quiet wrong-behavior — the offending plugin gets no signal and the op silently runs without the plugins it expected.

Resolution:
- Cap-hit -> ABORT + rollback (§8/§14 reverse-order) with a diagnostic naming the op/plugin chain, so the loop surfaces instead of hiding.
- DROP 'a plugin may deliberately re-enable plugins on a nested call' — it lets a runaway defeat its own backstop.
- Add a one-line spec note discouraging a plugin from re-triggering its own op (the §0 'plugins don't depend on each other's presence' already covers the mutual half).

Legit deep-but-bounded chains are unaffected (they never hit the cap). Thin edge; the change is mostly a disposition flip (suppress -> fail) plus deleting the hatch. Touches §6, §15 (revision-log entry).
title: Greenfield §6: recursion guard cap -> fail+rollback (not silent plugin-free); drop the re-enable hatch
